passages.explanation: Jesus goes to the Mount of Olives and returns to the temple early the next morning to teach. The scribes and Pharisees bring a woman caught in adultery and place her in front of everyone. They cite Moses' law commanding stoning for adultery and ask Jesus what he thinks. John makes clear this is a trap: if Jesus says to stone her, he contradicts his message of mercy; if he says to let her go, he contradicts Moses' law. Instead of answering, Jesus bends down and writes on the ground with his finger. What he wrote is never revealed.

When they keep pressing him, Jesus straightens up and delivers his response: whoever among them is without sin should throw the first stone. Then he bends down and writes on the ground again. He doesn't argue the law or defend the woman's actions — he redirects the entire situation onto the accusers' own consciences.

One by one, starting with the oldest, they all leave. Jesus is left alone with the woman. He asks where her accusers are — has no one condemned her? She says no one. Jesus says he doesn't condemn her either, and tells her to go and stop sinning. He doesn't declare her innocent — he shows mercy while still naming the sin.

Jesus declares 'I am the light of the world' — anyone who follows him won't walk in darkness. The Pharisees challenge him: you're testifying about yourself, which isn't valid. Jesus responds differently than in chapter 5 — here he says his self-testimony is valid because he knows where he came from and where he's going, and they don't. They judge by human standards; he doesn't judge at all, but if he did, his judgment would be true because he's not alone — the Father is with him. He points to their own law: two witnesses make valid testimony. He is one witness, the Father is the other.

They ask where his Father is. Jesus says they know neither him nor his Father — if they knew Jesus, they'd know the Father too. He says this in the temple treasury, and still no one arrests him because his hour hasn't come.

Jesus tells them he's going away and they'll look for him but die in their sins. They can't come where he's going. They wonder if he means he'll kill himself. Jesus draws a sharp division: they are from below, he is from above. They are of this world, he is not. Unless they believe that 'I am he,' they will die in their sins.

They ask directly: who are you? Jesus' answer is difficult to translate precisely, but amounts to: I've been telling you from the start. He has much to say and judge about them, but he speaks only what the Father told him. They don't understand he's talking about God. Jesus says that when they 'lift up the Son of Man' — a reference to the crucifixion — they'll recognise who he is. The Father has not left him alone because he always does what pleases the Father.
Many believe in him after hearing this. To those new believers, Jesus says: if you remain in my word, you are truly my disciples. You will know the truth, and the truth will set you free.

They push back: they're Abraham's descendants and have never been enslaved to anyone. Jesus says everyone who sins is a slave to sin. A slave doesn't have a permanent place in the household, but a son does. If the Son sets you free, you're truly free. He acknowledges they're Abraham's biological descendants but says they're trying to kill him because his word has no place in them. He speaks what he has seen with his Father; they do what they have learned from their father.

They claim Abraham as their father. Jesus says if they were really Abraham's children, they'd act like Abraham — but Abraham never tried to kill someone for telling the truth from God. They're doing the works of their real father. They insist their father is God. Jesus says if God were their father, they'd love him, since he came from God. He tells them their father is the devil — a murderer and liar from the beginning. They don't believe Jesus precisely because he tells the truth. He challenges them: which of you can prove I've sinned? If I'm telling the truth, why don't you believe? Anyone who belongs to God hears God's words. They don't hear because they don't belong to God.

They retaliate by calling Jesus a Samaritan and saying he has a demon. Jesus denies having a demon — he honours his Father while they dishonour him. He doesn't seek his own glory; there is one who seeks it and judges. Then he makes another extraordinary claim: anyone who keeps his word will never see death.

They take this as proof he's possessed: Abraham died, the prophets died — who does he think he is? Jesus says if he glorifies himself it means nothing; it's the Father who glorifies him, the same God they claim to know but don't. Then he says Abraham rejoiced to see his day. They're incredulous — he's not even fifty years old. Jesus responds with 'Before Abraham came into existence, I AM.' The phrase 'I AM' echoes the name God gave himself when speaking to Moses. They understand exactly what he's claiming and pick up stones to kill him for blasphemy. Jesus hides himself and leaves the temple.
